data.title: xAI looking to raise $10B in new funding, would value AI startup at $75B: report
data.description:
Generative artificial intelligence startup xAI is looking to raise $10B in new funding that would value the Elon Musk-led company at $75B, Bloomberg reported.

Previous investors Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, and Valor Equity Partners have been in discussions to participate in the new funding round, the news outlet added, citing a person familiar with the matter.

San Francisco-based xAI did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Maker of the Grok chatbot, xAI raised $6B in new funding late last year that valued the company at $50B. Some of that funding went towards xAI's massive data center in Memphis, where it will use 100,000 of Nvidia's (NVDA) H100 GPUs, Musk said in a post on X. The process to build it took 122 days.

In November, it was reported that the company was on track to generate $100M in annual revenue, though most of that was from Musk's other companies, including the social network X, previously known as Twitter.

One month prior, xAI released its first application programming interface, or API. Currently, the xAI API only has one model, known as “grok-beta.” This model is priced at $5M per million input tokens or $15M per million output tokens. It is unclear if the models are Grok-2 or Grok-2 mini, which were announced in August.

Last month, xAI, which competes with Microsoft (MSFT) backed OpenAI, Anthropic and others, released its official Grok app for iOS.

Musk said in July that he would bring the idea of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) investing $5B into xAI to the electric vehicle maker's board of directors after the idea received nearly two-thirds of “yes” votes in an online poll.

News of the new funding round comes as a Musk-led group made an unsolicited offer to buy the non-profit entity that controls ChatGPT maker OpenAI for $97.4B. Included in the group is xAI, according to a letter of intent sent to OpenAI's board of directors.

Some have speculated that the offer for OpenAI is nothing more than a ploy from Musk (who is currently suing OpenAI in an attempt to stop its for-profit conversion) to slow down OpenAI's capital raising.

data.title: Vera plunges as Otsuka updates on rival therapy
data.description:
Shares of Vera Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VERA) lost ~20% on Friday to reach a new 52-week low, in a move attributed to a potential market launch of a rival kidney disease therapy from Otsuka Holdings (OTCPK:OTSKF) as a once-monthly regimen.

Citing the Tokyo-based firm’s earnings results on Friday, Evercore ISI analyst Liisa Bayko argued that Otsuka’s (OTCPK:OTSKY) sibeprenlimab, which rivals Vera’s (NASDAQ:VERA) atacicept in the IgA nephropathy space, could reach the market “a few months ahead.”

While both drugs are in late-stage development for the immune disorder, with key data updates expected in June-July, Bayko said that sibeprenlimab is designed for less frequent dosing than atacicept, which requires weekly dosing.

Despite potential volatility ahead of the readouts, “We continue to recommend the stock as the profile is greatly derisked & will benefit from first-ish mover advantage in IgAN, a field anxiously awaiting these disease-modifying treatments,” Bayko, with an Outperform rating on VERA, wrote.

Goldman Sachs also reaffirmed its Buy rating on VERA, arguing that the selloff is an overreaction as Otsuka’s (OTCPK:OTSKY) disclosure more or less aligned with future milestones in the IgAN space.

The analyst Paul Choi awaits a topline readout in Q2 2025 from VERA’s Phase 3 ORIGIN trial for atacicept in IgAN closely followed by the company’s future regulatory filings for the drug.

data.title: Uber's EU rival Bolt to offer ride-hailing service in major Canadian cities - Bloomberg
data.description:
Uber-rival Bolt Technology is recruiting drivers ahead of launching its services in major Canadian cities, Bloomberg reported Friday, as it looks to boost its North American market share after recently launching its scooter rental business in D.C.

The Estonia-based company offers ride-hailing, food delivery, and scooter rentals in more than 45 countries in Europe, Africa, Asia, and Latin America.

In the U.S. and Canada, it is doing business as “Hopp.” The company branded itself as such to avoid confusion with a defunct company by Olympic gold medalist Usain Bolt.

The report said the company's driver recruitment site showed it is preparing to launch the service in Toronto, Mississauga, Markham, Vaughan, and Richmond Hill, the top five cities in Ontario, Canada.

Sequoia Capital and Fidelity Management led a €7.4B funding round for Bolt back in 2022.

data.title: Granite Point targets new loan originations by late 2025 with improving liquidity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Granite Point Mortgage Trust Inc. (NYSE:GPMT) Q4 2024

Management View

  • Granite Point's CEO, Jack Taylor, highlighted notable progress in resolving nonperforming loans in 2024, with $344 million resolved and $415 million in loan repayments and amortizations. He emphasized the company's proactive approach to asset and balance sheet management amid challenging market conditions.
  • Taylor noted improved liquidity in the commercial real estate market, particularly in the SASB, conduit, and CLO markets, though liquidity in the floating rate transitional middle market sector remains less robust.
  • The company repurchased 2.4 million common shares during 2024, with 1.2 million repurchased in Q4, reflecting management's belief that the stock is undervalued. Approximately 4.8 million shares remain under the current authorization.
  • CFO Blake Johnson reported a GAAP net loss of $42.4 million, or negative $0.86 per basic common share, for Q4. He stated that $37.2 million of the loss was attributed to credit loss provisions. Johnson highlighted a $0.78 decline in book value per share, ending at $8.47.
  • CIO Steve Alpart said the loan portfolio stood at $2.2 billion in total commitments, with a weighted average stabilized LTV of 64% at origination. He noted that the Q4 loan portfolio yield was 6.6%, net of nonaccrual loans.

Outlook

  • Management anticipates returning to new loan originations in the latter half of 2025, focusing on building a portfolio of earning assets to enhance run-rate profitability.
  • The company aims to resolve the remaining five rated loans, totaling $356 million, by mid-2025, though some may require extended timelines.
  • Liquidity improvements are expected through loan repayments and potential leveraging of REO assets, supporting flexibility for future opportunities.

Financial Results

  • Granite Point reported $303 million in loan repayments and resolutions during Q4, resulting in a net loan portfolio reduction of $243 million.
  • The company's CECL reserve declined by $58 million to $201 million at year-end 2024, driven by write-offs related to nonaccrual loan resolutions.
  • Cash balances ended at $88 million, with management aiming to further increase liquidity in early 2025.
  • The company emphasized its focus on maintaining financial flexibility and liquidity as it transitions to new originations later in the year.

Q&A

  • Douglas Harter, UBS: Asked about the rationale for leveraging REO properties. Jack Taylor responded that it is primarily to maintain liquidity, though no specific needs have been identified.
  • Steve DeLaney, Citizens JMP: Inquired about refinancing opportunities for Granite Point's CLOs. Taylor noted potential refinancing or asset combinations later in 2025, supported by improving CLO market conditions.
  • Jade Rahmani, KBW: Raised concerns about asset management and credit downgrades, particularly a long-held student housing loan in Kentucky. Taylor attributed the downgrade to a recent unfavorable arbitration conclusion but emphasized the company's proactive asset management approach.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism regarding the company's ability to prevent further loan migrations to risk-rated five and sought clarity on asset management practices. Questions often reflected concerns about liquidity, credit performance, and strategic priorities.
  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing progress in loan resolutions, improving market conditions, and undervaluation of the stock. During the Q&A, responses were detailed but defensive when addressing concerns about credit downgrades and asset management.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more focused on the timeline and risks associated with loan resolutions, while management maintained a consistent tone of cautious optimism.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from focusing on near-term loan resolutions to emphasizing new originations in late 2025.
  • Strategic priorities remain consistent, with ongoing focus on liquidity and resolving nonperforming loans, but management highlighted improving market conditions more prominently this quarter.
  • Analysts' tone reflected greater skepticism about asset management practices and credit migrations compared to Q3, where the focus was on operational progress.
  • Management sentiment remained steady, with confidence in the long-term outlook despite ongoing challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing risks related to nonperforming loans, particularly the five rated loans totaling $356 million. Resolutions are expected to extend into mid-2025 for some assets.
  • Analysts raised concerns about potential additional downgrades among four rated loans and the impact of higher treasury rates on portfolio performance.
  • Liquidity remains a priority, with management emphasizing flexibility through REO leveraging and cash reserves.

Final Takeaway

Granite Point Mortgage Trust Inc. outlined significant progress in resolving nonperforming loans while maintaining liquidity and flexibility. Management anticipates returning to new originations in late 2025, supported by improving market conditions. However, analysts expressed concerns about credit migrations and liquidity management, seeking clarity on the resolution timelines and asset strategies. Despite a challenging environment, the company emphasized its proactive approach to managing risks and enhancing shareholder value through opportunistic share buybacks and portfolio optimization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CAE Inc. projects $20B backlog growth amid strong pilot demand and defense opportunities
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CAE Inc. (NYSE:CAE) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Marc Parent highlighted a record $20.3 billion adjusted backlog, supported by $2.2 billion in new orders during the quarter. Civil segment backlog grew to $8.8 billion, a 44% year-over-year increase, with $1.5 billion in orders reflecting a 2x book-to-sales ratio. Defense backlog reached $11.5 billion, up 104% year-over-year.
  • Civil aviation operations saw revenue growth of 21%, driven by a notable increase in simulator product deliveries, rising from 13 in the same quarter last year to 20 this quarter. Business aviation training showed strong momentum, with increased profitability and higher utilization of newly deployed simulators.
  • Defense performance exceeded expectations with a book-to-sales ratio of 1.5x. Key contracts included Canadian Future Aircrew Training, U.S. Army fixed-wing training support, and updates for F-16 fighter training devices.
  • CFO Constantino Malatesta detailed record free cash flow of $409.8 million, driven by higher non-cash working capital contributions and net income. He noted that net debt to adjusted EBITDA is targeted to fall below 3x by fiscal year-end.

Outlook

  • Marc Parent indicated civil-adjusted segment operating income growth for the year is now expected to be modestly below the previously stated 10% due to commercial aircraft delivery delays. Annual Civil segment operating margins are also anticipated to fall slightly short of the 22%-23% range.
  • Defense revenue growth expectations were upgraded to high single-digit percentage growth per year, up from low to mid-single digits, with margins anticipated to exceed the previously guided 6%-7% range.
  • Parent emphasized long-term opportunities, with the global delivery of nearly 15,000 aircraft by Boeing and Airbus over the next decade supporting structural growth in pilot demand and training services.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue rose 12% year-over-year to $1.22 billion, with adjusted segment operating income increasing 31% to $190 million. Adjusted EPS stood at $0.29, up from $0.24 in the same quarter last year.
  • Civil segment revenue reached $752.6 million with a 20% adjusted operating margin, while defense revenue remained stable at $470.8 million, achieving an 8.3% operating margin.
  • Free cash flow of $409.8 million marked a significant year-over-year improvement. Capital expenditures totaled $97.6 million, primarily allocated to expanding the global training network.

Q&A

  • James McGarragle, RBC Capital Markets: Asked about the impact of potential tariffs. CEO Parent stated no material short-term impact is expected, highlighting CAE's ability to adapt due to its global services footprint. He noted that most revenue is generated locally and emphasized strong simulator sales outside the U.S. this year.
  • Fadi Chamoun, BMO Capital Markets: Inquired about civil aviation organic growth. Parent pointed to improvements in Boeing and Airbus deliveries and strong business aviation performance. COO Nick Leontidis added that utilization in Asia-Pacific and Europe remains high, offsetting U.S. softness.
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Asked about simulator delivery targets. Leontidis confirmed that the company remains on track for over 50 simulator deliveries in fiscal 2025, with Q4 expected to be particularly strong.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on the potential for recovery in commercial aviation and the long-term strength of the defense backlog. Concerns about aircraft delivery delays and U.S. pilot hiring trends were balanced by positive commentary on free cash flow and backlog growth.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing strategic execution and long-term opportunities. Parent reiterated, "We continue to foresee ample room for margin expansion in future years on volume, efficiency, and mix."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management demonstrated improved confidence, supported by record backlogs and cash flow performance, while acknowledging ongoing headwinds in commercial aviation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Adjusted segment operating income increased from $149 million in Q2 to $190 million in Q3, reflecting improved execution and higher simulator deliveries.
  • Defense margins rose to 8.3% in Q3 from 6.7% in Q2, driven by the completion of legacy contracts and improved execution.
  • Free cash flow surged to $409.8 million from $140 million in the previous quarter, supported by non-cash working capital contributions.
  • Management's outlook for civil operating income growth was tempered from Q2, reflecting continued commercial aviation softness in the Americas.

Risks and Concerns

  • Delays in commercial aircraft deliveries and ongoing supply chain challenges pose risks to near-term pilot training demand.
  • Lower pilot hiring activity in the U.S. impacted training utilization in the Americas, though management expects recovery as aircraft deliveries improve.
  • Geopolitical uncertainties and potential tariff impacts could affect defense and training product deliveries, although management highlighted mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

CAE Inc. demonstrated strong financial performance in Q3 2025, with record backlogs and free cash flow underscoring its solid position in both civil and defense markets. While challenges persist in commercial aviation, particularly in the Americas, the company remains optimistic about long-term growth opportunities driven by structural demand for pilot training and defense modernization. Management's focus on execution, cost discipline, and backlog quality supports confidence in achieving its strategic goals for the fiscal year and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hecla Mining outlines 2025 silver production target of up to 17M ounces amid strategic asset review
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hecla Mining Company (NYSE:HL) Q4 2024

Management View

  • President and CEO Rob Krcmarov expressed optimism regarding Hecla’s future, emphasizing the company’s robust operational foundation and management quality. He highlighted the company’s key strategic pillars, including operational excellence through cost control, portfolio optimization, disciplined capital allocation, and a focus on silver production in North America.

  • He stated that Greens Creek and Lucky Friday remain cornerstone operations, generating significant cash flow, while Keno Hill faces challenges but holds strong growth potential. The strategic review of Casa Berardi continues, aiming to maximize shareholder value through all potential alternatives.

  • CFO Russell Lawlar noted key financial achievements, including $228 million in free cash flow from Greens Creek and Lucky Friday in 2024 and a significant improvement in the net leverage ratio from 2.7x to 1.6x. He also announced the elimination of the silver-linked dividend to prioritize investments in organic growth and deleveraging.

  • Lawlar highlighted record revenues of over $900 million in 2024, supported by strong contributions from all operating mines and a 23% increase in the realized price of silver.

  • COO Carlos Aguiar discussed operational highlights, particularly record-breaking performance at Lucky Friday and steady production at Greens Creek, despite challenges such as equipment issues and higher labor costs.

Outlook

  • Silver production for 2025 is projected between 15.5 million and 17 million ounces, with gold production expected to decline due to Casa Berardi transitioning to open-pit mining only.

  • Greens Creek’s 2025 production is anticipated to range from 8.1 million to 8.8 million ounces of silver, while Lucky Friday is expected to produce between 4.7 million and 5.1 million ounces of silver. Keno Hill is targeting production of 2.7 million to 3.1 million ounces of silver.

  • Capital investments are projected to increase slightly in 2025 to sustain production at core assets and advance growth initiatives at Keno Hill and Casa Berardi.

Financial Results

  • The company reported record revenues of over $900 million for 2024, with contributions of 44% from silver, 34% from gold, and the remainder from base metals. Copper revenue was also generated for the first time in decades.

  • Greens Creek produced 8.5 million ounces of silver for the year, generating $147 million in free cash flow despite operational challenges. Lucky Friday achieved record-breaking production, generating $82 million in free cash flow, including $50 million in insurance receipts.

  • Keno Hill met its 2024 production guidance with 2.8 million ounces of silver but faced operational and social challenges, including a 25-day mill shutdown. Casa Berardi produced 87,000 ounces of gold in 2024, with the ongoing transition to open-pit mining expected to improve cash flow starting mid-2025.

Q&A

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley Securities: Questioned the timeline for Casa Berardi’s strategic review and the potential for divestment. CEO Rob Krcmarov responded that all options, including divestment, are being considered, with a clearer view expected in Q2 2025.

  • Kevin O’Halloran, BMO Capital Markets: Inquired about Keno Hill’s permitting timeline and infrastructure upgrades. Krcmarov noted positive momentum in discussions with the Yukon government and highlighted a two-phase strategy to achieve 600 tons per day throughput.

  • Joseph Reagor, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about potential cost savings from Keno Hill’s planned mining method changes. COO Carlos Aguiar emphasized safety as the primary goal, with cost reductions to be evaluated as implementation progresses.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with questions focused on strategic reviews, operational challenges, and cost management. Concerns centered on permitting delays at Keno Hill and the financial implications of Casa Berardi’s production gap.

  • Management displayed a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing operational achievements and financial discipline. However, their tone during the Q&A was slightly defensive when addressing challenges at Keno Hill and Casa Berardi, reflecting ongoing uncertainties.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, confidence levels improved slightly, particularly regarding financial performance and deleveraging progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 reflects a consistent silver production outlook and increased capital investment compared to the previous quarter. The strategic focus on North American assets remains unchanged, but the review of Casa Berardi represents a potential shift in portfolio strategy.

  • Analysts’ questions this quarter showed greater focus on specific operational challenges at Keno Hill and Casa Berardi compared to broader strategic discussions last quarter.

  • Management demonstrated an improved tone regarding financial performance but maintained a cautious approach to addressing operational hurdles.

Risks and Concerns

  • Permitting delays at Keno Hill pose a significant risk to achieving production targets and cost efficiency improvements. Management is engaging with the Yukon government and First Nations to address these challenges.

  • Casa Berardi faces a five-year production gap following the transition to open-pit mining, raising concerns about its long-term viability within the portfolio.

  • Higher labor and power costs across operations are expected to impact 2025 cost per ounce metrics.

Final Takeaway

Hecla Mining Company delivered record revenues and strong cash flow in 2024, driven by robust performance at Greens Creek and Lucky Friday. However, challenges persist at Keno Hill and Casa Berardi, with management focused on addressing permitting delays and evaluating strategic alternatives. The company projects consistent silver production for 2025, supported by increased capital investments in core assets and growth initiatives, while maintaining a disciplined approach to financial flexibility and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: WK Kellogg gains amid report of Ferrero takeover interest
data.description:
WK Kellogg (NYSE:KLG) rose 10% amid a report that Ferrero is evaluating a potential takeover.

Ferrero, the family-backed maker of brands such as Nutella, is in the early stages of considering a potential bid for Kellogg (NYSE:KLG), according to a Dealreporter item on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

The deliberations are very early and there's no guarantee any bid will be made.

Developing story ...

data.title: Applied DNA signals growth with GMP facility launch and LineaRx focus in 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Applied DNA Sciences (NASDAQ:APDN) Q1 2025

Management View

  • The company announced a strategic restructuring to focus on its LineaRx subsidiary, exiting the DNA Tagging and Security Products and Services segment. This move is expected to bolster cash reserves and optimize costs to support synthetic DNA manufacturing, with a workforce reduction of approximately 20%.
  • Chairman & CEO James Hayward highlighted the operational readiness of the GMP Site 1 facility, certified as an ISO 7 space with ISO 5 workspaces, marking a milestone in enzymatic DNA production. He emphasized that LineaRx’s platforms are positioned to meet growing market demand for plasmid DNA alternatives.
  • CFO Beth Jantzen reported total revenues of $1.2 million for Q1 2025, a year-over-year increase driven by higher product revenue from DNA Tagging and increased isotopic testing services. Operating loss for the quarter was $3 million, an improvement from $3.8 million in the prior year, attributed to lower selling, general, and administrative costs.
  • The company appointed Judy Murrah as President and Clay Shorrock as President of LineaRx to drive operational excellence and business growth.
  • Shorrock outlined plans to monetize demand for GMP IVT templates, targeting revenues ranging from $50,000 to $1 million per opportunity for IVT templates and up to $3 million when paired with Linea RNAP under the Linea IVT platform.

Outlook

  • Management aims to expand the GMP manufacturing capacity to meet demand from mRNA therapeutics developers and CDMOs, with contracts expected to materialize in the May-June timeframe.
  • The company unveiled Linea donor DNA, a new product targeting CRISPR gene-editing applications, expected to be available in GMP grade later in 2025.
  • Plans include hiring a VP-level industry leader to lead a new sales team and upgrading GMP capabilities to enable manufacturing for various clinical applications, including CAR T manufacturing and personalized DNA vaccines.

Financial Results

  • Total revenues increased to $1.2 million in Q1 2025 from $891,000 in the prior year. This growth was attributed to higher product revenue from DNA Tagging and increased isotopic testing services.
  • The company reported an operating loss of $3 million, an improvement from $3.8 million in the prior year, due to lower SG&A costs and reduced stock-based compensation.
  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at $9.3 million as of December 31, 2024, up from $6.4 million on September 30, 2024, supported by an October financing and warrant exercises.
  • Adjusted EBITDA improved to negative $2.9 million compared to negative $3.2 million in the prior year.

Q&A

  • Eduardo Arce, H.C. Wainwright: Asked about revenue growth drivers and whether they were tied to new customer acquisitions or scaling existing customers. CFO Beth Jantzen explained that growth was driven by a shipment of DNA Tagging for cotton marking and increased isotopic testing services revenue. Clay Shorrock added that the cotton contract was retained post-restructuring due to its compatibility with LineaRx workflows.
  • Eduardo Arce, H.C. Wainwright: Inquired about the revenue potential of the AML therapy trial using Linea DNA. Shorrock noted that while the trial is hospital-based with limited revenue opportunity, it validated Linea DNA’s regulatory approval and competitive advantages over plasmid DNA in CAR T workflows.
  • Eduardo Arce, H.C. Wainwright: Sought details on the market opportunity for Linea donor DNA in CRISPR therapies. Shorrock emphasized the significant potential in cell and gene therapy, particularly CAR T applications, and the scalability of existing workflows to meet donor DNA demand.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in the company’s LineaRx platforms and market opportunities, with a neutral to slightly positive tone during the Q&A. Questions focused on revenue drivers and the potential of new products.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing strategic restructuring, operational milestones, and growth plans. CEO James Hayward highlighted the company’s readiness to meet market demand, while Clay Shorrock and Judy Murrah detailed product opportunities and operational capabilities.
  • Compared to the prepared remarks, management displayed consistent confidence in both sections, aligning their answers with their restructuring strategy and growth expectations.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • A direct quarter-over-quarter comparison is unavailable due to the lack of prior transcript data.
  • Shifts in strategic focus were evident with the exit from the DNA Tagging segment and full commitment to LineaRx’s enzymatic DNA production capabilities.
  • Management’s tone was optimistic regarding LineaRx’s growth trajectory and operational readiness, with a clear emphasis on achieving profitability through disciplined spending and scalable manufacturing.
  • Analysts concentrated on the monetization of new products and the financial impact of restructuring, reflecting a keen interest in the viability of LineaRx as a growth driver.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Beth Jantzen highlighted the company’s substantial doubt regarding its ability to continue as a going concern, dependent on implementing the business plan and raising additional capital.
  • Management is mitigating risks by restructuring, optimizing costs, and aligning CapEx with commercial contracts to reduce balance sheet risk.
  • Analysts raised concerns about revenue scalability and the timeline for realizing returns on new therapies and products.

Final Takeaway

Applied DNA Sciences is undergoing a strategic transformation, exiting non-core segments to focus on its LineaRx subsidiary and GMP manufacturing capabilities. With the successful certification of its GMP Site 1 facility and the launch of new products like Linea donor DNA, the company is positioned to address growing demand in biotherapeutics and gene editing markets. However, challenges remain, including revenue scalability and the need to secure additional capital to address its going concern status.
